my_sentence: Today I was fine.
correction: I had a good day.
explanation: When someone asks "How was your day?" they are asking whether you enjoyed it, so answer with "I had a good/bad day" or "It was stressful." "I was fine" answers "How are you?", not "How was your day?".
correct_examples: I had a good day, thanks.
It was a stressful day.
context: Teacher asking how his day went

my_sentence: And had some meetings with my coworkers.
correction: I had a meeting with my coworkers.
explanation: An English clause needs an explicit subject; don't drop "I". Here it was also one meeting, so use the singular "a meeting".
correct_examples: I had a meeting with my coworkers today.
I attended a meeting this morning.
context: Describing his workday

my_sentence: Customers can use their electronic money to pay in our shopping mall.
correction: ... whenever we buy something at the shopping mall.
explanation: Use "at" for a place where an activity happens (at the shopping mall, at the station). "in the shopping mall" focuses on being physically inside; for shopping/paying, "at" is the natural choice.
correct_examples: I bought it at the shopping mall.
You can pay at any store.
context: Explaining the payment system

my_sentence: It is very important system to the company.
correction: It is a very important system to the company.
explanation: A singular countable noun (system) needs an article. Use "a" before "very important system".
correct_examples: It is a very important system.
This is a key part of the company.
context: Talking about the payment system's importance

my_sentence: We need to collect some informations about our superior.
correction: We need to collect some information about our superior.
explanation: "Information" is uncountable and has no plural form. Never say "informations"; use "some information" or "pieces of information".
correct_examples: I collected some information about them.
He gave me a lot of useful information.
context: How to impress your superiors

my_sentence: Because they will find you can help them a lot.
correction: They will find that you help them a lot.
explanation: After "find" reporting a fact, use the conjunction "that" to introduce the clause, and use the simple present "help" (not "can help") for a general truth.
correct_examples: They will find that you help them a lot.
She realized that he was reliable.
context: Explaining how you impress superiors

my_sentence: We can prepare some documents and solutions to talk with them.
correction: ... to discuss the topics with them.
explanation: "Discuss" takes a direct object (discuss the topics), while "talk" needs a preposition (talk about something). Here "discuss the topics with them" is the natural collocation.
correct_examples: We discussed the plan with the manager.
Let's discuss the details tomorrow.
context: Steps to impress superiors

my_sentence: So when they meet some problems, they will remind us.
correction: If they encounter some of these problems, they will remind us immediately.
explanation: We don't "meet" a problem; the natural collocations are "encounter / run into / come across a problem". "Meet" is for people.
correct_examples: If you encounter any problems, let me know.
We ran into a few issues during testing.
context: Benefits of preparing for superiors

my_sentence: According to the collect information you can get, you can understand the company's next actions.
correction: According to the collected information ...
explanation: Use the past participle "collected" as an adjective before the noun (the collected information = information that has been collected), not the base verb "collect".
correct_examples: Based on the collected data, we made a decision.
The gathered information was very useful.
context: Using information to understand the company

my_sentence: You can understand this thing.
correction: You can understand these things.
explanation: Match the demonstrative to the noun's number. For a plural noun use "these things"; "this thing" is singular.
correct_examples: These things take time.
I can handle these tasks.
context: Understanding company information

my_sentence: It's very useful. You can get a lot.
correction: You can use it a lot, not get it.
explanation: "Get" means to obtain/receive; "use" means to make use of. A system that is useful is one you "use a lot", not "get a lot".
correct_examples: This tool is useful; you can use it a lot.
We use this platform every day.
context: Talking about a system serving millions of users

my_sentence: I want to do something that make me happy.
correction: I want to do something that makes me happy.
explanation: In a relative clause, the verb agrees with the antecedent. "Something" is singular, so use "makes" (something that makes me happy).
correct_examples: I want a job that makes me happy.
She needs something that works for her.
context: What he wants in personal life

my_sentence: It's tired and boring. We need to do something relax.
correction: It's tiring and boring. We need to do something relaxing.
explanation: Use the -ing adjective for what causes a feeling (a tiring/relaxing activity) and the -ed adjective for the person who feels it (I am tired). "Relax" is a verb; the adjective is "relaxing".
correct_examples: The trip was relaxing.
That meeting was tiring, so I felt tired afterwards.
context: Impressing others is tiring; wanting relaxing activities

my_sentence: His behavior is so naive and his speaking was so terrible.
correction: His behavior was so naive and his verbal expressions were so terrible.
explanation: When narrating a past first impression, keep the verbs in the past tense ("was", "were") consistently, not the present "is".
correct_examples: His behavior was very immature.
Back then, she was very shy.
context: Describing a coworker's bad first impression

my_sentence: His speaking was so terrible.
correction: His verbal expressions were so terrible.
explanation: "Speaking" refers to the skill/act of talking in a language; to describe how someone communicates, say "verbal expression" or "the way he speaks/communicates".
correct_examples: His verbal expression was poor.
The way he communicates is unclear.
context: Describing a coworker

my_sentence: We were neighbored in the countryside.
correction: We were neighbours in the countryside.
explanation: "Neighbour" is a noun (the person next door). There is no common verb "to neighbor" here; say "we were neighbours".
correct_examples: We were neighbours for ten years.
They are my neighbours.
context: Describing an old friend from childhood

my_sentence: He regarded everyone as a idiot.
correction: He regarded everyone as an idiot.
explanation: Use "an" before a word starting with a vowel sound (idiot). "A idiot" is incorrect.
correct_examples: She treated him like an idiot.
He is an honest man.
context: Describing an arrogant CTO

my_sentence: Everyone don't like him.
correction: Everyone doesn't like him. / Nobody likes him.
explanation: "Everyone" is grammatically singular, so it takes "doesn't". A more natural way to express this idea is "Nobody likes him."
correct_examples: Everyone here knows the rule.
Nobody likes working overtime.
context: Describing the arrogant CTO

my_sentence: Many of people resigned because of him.
correction: Many of the people resigned because of him. / Many people resigned because of him.
explanation: "Many of" must be followed by a determiner: "many of the people". Without "of", just say "many people".
correct_examples: Many of the employees left.
Many people agreed with the plan.
context: Effect of the arrogant CTO

my_sentence: He was not working there anymore.
correction: He is not working there anymore.
explanation: For a state that is still true now (he no longer works there), use the present "is not working", not the past "was".
correct_examples: He doesn't work here anymore.
She is not living in Beijing anymore.
context: Whether the CTO still works there

my_sentence: They use their beautiful to make money.
correction: They use their beauty to make money.
explanation: "Beautiful" is an adjective; the noun is "beauty". You need a noun as the object of "use".
correct_examples: They use their beauty to sell products.
Her beauty is famous.
context: Talking about the modeling/fashion industry

my_sentence: They don't have enough technology companies.
correction: They don't have enough technological companies.
explanation: Use the adjective "technological" to describe "companies". "Technology companies" exists but the teacher pointed out the adjective form is what's needed to modify the noun here.
correct_examples: It is a technological breakthrough.
The region lacks technological infrastructure.
context: Why he wouldn't move to the Netherlands

my_sentence: Nowadays I deploy application.
correction: Nowadays I deploy an application.
explanation: A singular countable noun (application) needs an article: "deploy an application". For the general activity you could also say "deploy applications".
correct_examples: I deploy an application every week.
We deploy applications to the cloud.
context: Talking about his job and switching to AI

my_sentence: It's a very big, important department.
correction: It's a very big and important department.
explanation: When two adjectives both directly describe the noun, join them with "and" (big and important) rather than only a comma in speech.
correct_examples: It's a big and important project.
She is a kind and patient teacher.
context: Describing the CTO's former department at Sony
